nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, rocky sediment-free habitat along the margins of Lake Malawi; males hold territories over open rock faces and feed on aufwuchs (algae and associated invertebrates) attached to rocky substrate

decorPreferences: large rocky structures with caves and overhangs for male territory definition
open sand areas between rock groups for females and subdominant fish
robust plants such as Anubias and Java fern if desired — plants not grazed but may be uprooted during spawning
strong filtration and high turnover to replicate Lake Malawi’s near-oligotrophic clarity

diet.notes: Insectivore-leaning omnivore. Wild diet centres on aufwuchs — algae and small invertebrates (zooplankton, crustaceans, insect larvae) scraped from rock faces. In aquaria, a spirulina-based cichlid flake or pellet should form the dietary staple, supplemented with frozen mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, daphnia, and krill. Avoid high-protein mammalian-meat foods (beef heart, worm-heavy diets) which promote Malawi Bloat. Feed two to three small portions daily rather than one large meal.

goodWith: other large Lake Malawi Haplochromines of comparable size (Copadichromis, Sciaenochromis, Placidochromis)
large Synodontis catfish (S. multipunctatus, S. petricola)
Labidochromis caeruleus (Yellow Lab) and other non-aggressive mbuna in large tanks
avoidWith: aggressive mbuna (Melanochromis, Pseudotropheus crabro) — excessive harassment of females
conspecific males without sufficient tank length — intense male-male aggression at breeding
different locality forms or colour morphs of P. taeniolatus (hybridisation risk)
South American and Central American cichlids (incompatible water chemistry)
small fish and nano species (may be consumed or harassed)
invertebrates such as shrimp and snails (eaten)

imageCandidates.notes: Adult male showing typical red body with blue-green head and yellow caudal region. FishBase species photo for P. taeniolatus.
Adult male lateral profile; confirm license and attribution on Wikimedia page before use.

summary: The Red Empress is a spectacular Lake Malawi Haplochromine in which dominant males display a vivid spectrum — red-orange flanks, a blue-green iridescent head, and a yellow-edged caudal fin. Endemic to Lake Malawi’s rocky shorelines, it grazes aufwuchs and is a maternal mouthbrooder. Multiple locality forms (Red Empress, Tanzania, Likoma) exist and should not be mixed.
careNotes:
Protomelas taeniolatus is one of the most visually rewarding of the Lake Malawi Haplochromines. Dominant males develop a striking three-tone coloration — a red to burnt-orange body, a metallic blue or blue-green head, and yellow pigmentation in the upper and lower caudal-fin lobes. Females and subdominant males are cryptically coloured in silver-grey with faint lateral banding and are easily overlooked at a fish store. Full male colour typically develops by 9–12 months and intensifies further in the presence of females or rival males.

Several recognised locality forms exist in the hobby, most commonly the “Red Empress” (broadly distributed form), the “Tanzania Red” or “Tanzania” form, and the “Likoma Island” form. These forms differ subtly in base coloration and fin patterning. They must not be housed together as hybridisation is readily achieved and produces non-true offspring that flood the hobby; always purchase from a reputable source with clearly labelled locality provenance.

A minimum 300 L tank with a 150 cm footprint is required for a single male with two or three females. A 400–500 L display is strongly recommended for a mixed Hap–Peacock community. Water chemistry must mirror Lake Malawi: hard (gH 10–25), alkaline (pH 7.6–8.8), and well-buffered. Temperature of 24–28°C is appropriate. Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are essential. Maintain high water movement and efficient filtration; Lake Malawi is one of the world’s largest and clearest lakes — dissolved waste accumulates quickly in aquaria.

Rock formations along the back and sides of the tank with open sandy areas in the midground replicate the species’ natural rocky-shoreline habitat. Males claim a rock territory and court females through lateral display and circling. Only one male per tank is advised unless the tank is 500 L or more with substantial visual barriers between rock zones. Malawi Bloat (cichlid bloat, caused by Hexamita and secondary bacterial infection exacerbated by poor diet and water quality) is the principal disease risk; a spirulina-dominant diet with restricted protein prevents most cases.
breedingNotes:
P. taeniolatus is a maternal mouthbrooder. Spawning typically occurs on a flat rock or sandy patch within the male’s territory. The female deposits 40–60 eggs which she immediately collects in her buccal cavity; fertilisation occurs as the female mouths the male’s egg-spot markings on his anal fin (the classic cichlid “egg-spot” spawning behaviour). The female then withdraws and ceases feeding for the incubation period.

Eggs hatch within the female’s mouth and are brooded for approximately 21–28 days at 26–28°C. Released fry are fully formed and free-swimming at 8–10 mm; they are capable of accepting freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii, baby brine shrimp, micro-pellets, and crushed spirulina flake from the first day of release. Brood size is typically 30–50 fry. To maximise survival, the holding female should be moved to a separate brooding vessel at around day 18–20 (just before release) or allowed to release fry in a heavily decorated species tank. In a community Hap display, most fry will be eaten. A 1:2 or 1:3 male-to-female ratio substantially reduces post-spawn harassment of spent females. Breedability in captivity is relatively straightforward for an experienced cichlid keeper, given appropriate water conditions and group composition.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Protomelas taeniolatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (to 20 cm SL), temperature range (24–28°C), pH (7.6–8.8), hardness (10–25°H), minimum tank dimensions (120 × 45 × 37.5 cm / 200 L baseline), diet (vegetable-based with frozen supplement; protein-heavy foods cause obesity), temperament, locality-form notes (Red Empress, Super Red Empress, Blue Fire forms must not be mixed due to hybridisation), and breeding (maternal mouthbrooder, ~21 days incubation). Adult size reconciled with brief spec (~17 cm) using 20 cm as literature maximum and 12 cm as typical captive minimum.

additional.name: FishBase — Protomelas taeniolatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (11.3 cm TL recorded; wild fish larger), temperature (24–26°C), taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes, Pseudocrenilabrinae), native range (endemic Lake Malawi, widely distributed), habitat (shallow rocky sediment-free), trophic level 3.2 (omnivore), IUCN Least Concern (assessed June 2018). FishBase TL figure (11.3 cm) appears to be an aquarium-measured specimen; SeriouslyFish field data (to 20 cm SL) used for maximum size.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World — Red Empress Cichlid
additional.notes: Supplementary trade source. Confirmed size range (4.5–6 inches / ~11–15 cm captive), lifespan (~5 years minimum), temperature (23–28°C), pH (7.7–8.6), hardness (5–15 dGH), tank size (75–100 gal / ~285–380 L), mouthbrooder (3–4 week incubation, 40–50 eggs), semi-aggressive temperament, diet (spirulina-based pellets with frozen krill/mysis supplement). Lifespan extended to 8 years upper bound based on SeriouslyFish and general Hap longevity data.

additional.name: Cichlid-Forum — Lake Malawi Haplochromine community resources
additional.notes: Community expert resources used to corroborate locality-form provenance (Red Empress, Tanzania, Likoma Island forms), hybridisation warnings, 1:2–1:3 male-to-female ratio for breeding groups, and Malawi Bloat dietary risk factors. No single species article accessible directly; general Hap section used.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates 40–60 eggs buccally for 21–28 days and releases free-swimming fry at ~8–10 mm. Achievable in home aquaria with a 1:2–1:3 male-to-female ratio and a 300 L+ tank. Holding female ideally moved to a separate brooding vessel for fry protection. Fry accept BBS and micro-pellets from release.
